Case title: QDA delegation preferences: human-only versus human-initiated versus AI-initiated coding
Short description: Study how qualitative researchers choose among delegation levels in coding and thematic analysis.
Empirical unit of analysis: Interview data from qualitative researchers about preferred delegation levels
Candidate roles tested: Context witness; protocol architect; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Should context witness be separated from protocol architecture and ownership in qualitative analysis?
Observable artifacts / traces: Preference interviews, scenario rankings, trust/ownership themes
Key outcomes observed: Delegation preferences, ownership concerns, trust thresholds, perceived safe-use zones
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Researchers show no meaningful distinction between delegation levels or between ownership and context concerns
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Self-reported preferences may not match real delegated practice
Why this case has high leverage: It is one of the best cases for role splitting inside qualitative analysis without requiring live fieldwork.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
